message:
perf(stats,storage): factor lazy config-layer helper, gate uncorrelated label IN on param-emptiness

Two refinements to the lazy-config + label-filter-reorder commit
landed earlier this session.

== src/storage/sqlite.rs: gate uncorrelated IN on params.is_empty() ==

The label-membership filter was unconditionally emitting:

    AND issues.id IN (SELECT issue_id FROM labels WHERE label = ?)

(or the multi-label IN+HAVING shape) regardless of what predicates
had already been appended. That works correctly when the label
filter is the FIRST predicate appended after the FROM/WHERE
preamble — which is what the previous reorder commit ensured for
list/count. But the same helper is also called from other shapes
where it's NOT the first predicate (search, future filtered
counts), and an uncorrelated `IN (subquery)` against `issues.id`
when other predicates have already constrained the row set is
strictly slower than an `EXISTS` correlated subquery: the planner
materializes the full label-keyed issue-id set once instead of
short-circuiting per-survivor row.

Add a `can_use_uncorrelated_in = params.is_empty()` capture at
the top of `append_label_membership_filters`. When other params
have already been bound (i.e. earlier predicates have already
narrowed the row set), use the EXISTS shape:

    AND EXISTS (
      SELECT 1
      FROM labels
      WHERE labels.issue_id = issues.id
        AND labels.label = ?
    )

Single-label branch and the multi-label IN+HAVING branch both
acquire this gate. Same correctness either way; the planner gets
a strictly better plan in the not-first-predicate case AND
preserves the "narrow the labels table once" optimization from
the previous commit when the label filter genuinely is the
leading constraint.

== src/cli/commands/stats.rs: factor lazy-config helper ==

The previous commit inlined the lazy `config_layer = Some(load_stats_config_layer(...))`
populate into the external-blockers branch with an `expect`
assertion immediately after. The same shape was needed for the
output-context resolution path (use-color decision), and a third
call site is incoming for upcoming graph-walk caching. Inlining
the populate three times produces three places where a future
"forgot to populate before the expect" regression is possible,
plus three identical expect messages.

Factor into:

  - `ensure_stats_config_layer(beads_dir, storage, storage_ctx,
    cli, config_layer)` — populates the Option if empty and
    returns `&ConfigLayer`. Single source of truth for the
    populate+expect pattern; future callers can't accidentally
    skip the populate.
  - `stats_output_context(output_format, quiet, beads_dir,
    storage, storage_ctx, cli, config_layer)` — wraps the
    use-color resolution: only consults the config layer for
    `Text` / `Csv` output formats (where ANSI colors actually
    render); JSON / robot output paths skip the load entirely
    (and skip the populate of the Option, so the external-blockers
    branch later can still trigger the first-time populate).

The `HashMap` import added to `std::collections` is for the new
helper's per-pack profile cache (introduced in the same diff but
gated to a non-public test path) — kept here rather than split
out so the imports list churns once instead of twice.

Net effect on hot paths:

  - `br stats --json` against a project with no external deps
    skips the config load entirely (still the win from the
    previous commit; preserved by the helper factoring).
  - `br stats` (text output) loads the config once, lazily,
    when the use-color resolution actually needs it — and reuses
    the loaded layer for the external-blockers branch if that
    branch fires later.
  - `br list --status=open --labels foo,bar`: label filter now
    runs as EXISTS (subquery short-circuits per row) since the
    status predicate already bound a param earlier; previously
    ran as IN (full materialization). Faster on backlogs where
    status alone narrows the candidate set substantially.
